title: Neurogenic Bladder: Assessment and Management
objectives: Classify neurogenic bladder by neurologic level
Apply management strategies to preserve renal function

sections.title: Classification
sections.content:
**Upper Motor Neuron (Above S2-S4):**
• Spastic/hyperreflexic bladder
• Detrusor overactivity
• DSD (detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia)
• Small capacity, high pressure

**Lower Motor Neuron (S2-S4 or below):**
• Flaccid/areflexic bladder
• Large capacity, low pressure
• Overflow incontinence

**Causes:**
• Spinal cord injury
• Spina bifida/myelomeningocele
• Multiple sclerosis
• Stroke
• Parkinson's disease

sections.title: Evaluation
sections.content:
**Goals:**
• Protect upper tracts (kidneys)
• Achieve continence
• Preserve quality of life

**Workup:**
• Urodynamic study (essential)
• Renal ultrasound
• Serum creatinine
• Voiding diary

**Urodynamic Parameters:**
• Capacity, compliance
• Detrusor pressure
• Presence of DO or DSD
• Leak point pressure

**Danger Zone:** Leak point pressure >40 cm H2O → upper tract risk

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Goals:** Low pressure storage, adequate emptying

**Clean Intermittent Catheterization:**
• Gold standard for emptying
• 4-6 times daily
• Reduces UTI risk vs indwelling

**Anticholinergics:**
• Oxybutynin, tolterodine, solifenacin
• Reduce detrusor overactivity
• Side effects: Dry mouth, constipation, cognitive effects

**Beta-3 Agonists:**
• Mirabegron
• Alternative to anticholinergics

**Botulinum Toxin:**
• Intradetrusor injection
• For refractory DO
• Lasts 6-9 months

sections.title: Surgical Options
sections.content:
**Augmentation Cystoplasty:**
• Increases bladder capacity
• Using intestinal segment
• For refractory low compliance

**Sphincter Procedures:**
• Artificial urinary sphincter
• Sling procedures
• For stress incontinence

**Urinary Diversion:**
• Continent catheterizable stoma (Mitrofanoff)
• Ileal conduit for severe cases

**Sacral Neuromodulation:**
• For incomplete injuries
• Modulates bladder function

keyTakeaways: Upper vs lower motor neuron lesions have distinct bladder patterns
Leak point pressure >40 threatens upper tracts
CIC is gold standard for bladder emptying
Regular surveillance for hydronephrosis and UTI

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with T10 spinal cord injury has incontinence. Urodynamics show small capacity with high-pressure detrusor contractions and elevated leak point pressure. What is the most important initial management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: This patient has upper motor neuron neurogenic bladder with dangerous high pressures. CIC ensures regular emptying while anticholinergics reduce detrusor overactivity and pressure, protecting the upper tracts.
